distinctiveness: Local continuation that preserves the document’s explanatory tone.
rationale: Continues the immediately preceding sentence in the Turkey section.
text: failed to attract popular support to the new regime. Some of the key ministers such as Foreign Minister Sarper will probably remain in the new cabinet. (Page 1)

distinctiveness: Changes the main mechanism from minister selection to opposition mobilization.
rationale: Shifts emphasis toward opposition actors shaping public opinion.
text: in large part because opposition leaders abroad and dissatisfied unions had been able to exploit the election gap. The convocation timetable may thus be influenced less by constitutional practice than by bargaining among party bosses in Ankara.

distinctiveness: Introduces a risk-driven explanation involving unrest and army pressure.
rationale: Adds heightened instability tied to demonstrations and security strain.
text: failed to prevent growing street demonstrations, and several provincial CNU organizers have reported that security forces are already strained by labor unrest. If the new Constituent Assembly is delayed, President Gursel may have to broaden the cabinet with figures acceptable to army elements.

distinctiveness: Softens severity and treats the issue as communications/administrative performance.
rationale: Reframes impact as limited and primarily reputational.
text: failed to attract popular support largely because technicians lacked visible accomplishments, but there is no evidence that this has threatened the regime’s basic stability. The new cabinet is expected to restore confidence mainly through administrative continuity and better communication with party supporters.

distinctiveness: Shifts hypothesis toward external diplomacy and alliance reassurance.
rationale: Connects ministerial choices to foreign-policy messaging.
text: failed to attract popular support to the new regime. Some of the key ministers such as Foreign Minister Sarper will probably remain in the new cabinet. President Gursel may also seek to reassure Western and Balkan partners by retaining experienced foreign policy figures while presenting the reshuffle as a step toward political pluralism.

distinctiveness: Changes the key actor to the armed forces and a security-minded cabinet selection.
rationale: Introduces military confidence as the driver of cabinet composition.
text: failed because junior officers became convinced that civilian technicians would not reform internal discipline. As a result, President Gursel is likely to include at least one or two officers affiliated with the Committee of National Union to reassure the armed forces and prevent covert opposition within the barracks.

distinctiveness: Alters the mechanism from professional experience to party power and patronage.
rationale: Re-centers the explanation on party consolidation and patronage.
text: failed to attract popular support because the technicians’ program was perceived as overcentralized and insufficiently responsive to party demands. The new Constituent Assembly provides a vehicle for consolidating the CNU’s control by rewarding influential politicians and reducing the role of technocrats in day-to-day decisions.

distinctiveness: Focuses the cause on provincial economic performance and implementation logistics.
rationale: Adds an economic delivery/logistics rationale for popularity.
text: failed to attract popular support because economic measures announced during the previous period were slow in reaching the provinces. President Gursel may select ministers with ties to finance and regional administration so that new budgets and public works can be presented promptly to the Assembly.

distinctiveness: Emphasizes conflicting reporting and lack of clarity about depth of change.
rationale: Frames the interpretation as uncertain and contested by observers.
text: failed to attract popular support, although the extent is disputed among Ankara observers. Some press reports stress that the new cabinet will be no more than a change of personnel, while other reports suggest that Gursel intends a deeper political realignment within the Constituent Assembly.

distinctiveness: Shifts from popularity/technicians to constitutional/legal power-management as the real purpose.
rationale: Provides a document-level alternative: constitutional maneuver to constrain rivals.
text: failed to attract popular support to the new regime. The resignation and convocation schedule appear to be primarily a constitutional maneuver designed to neutralize legal challenges rather than to improve governance. Gursel may use the reshuffle to limit the influence of rival factions within the Assembly and thereby reduce the likelihood of early amendments that could constrain executive authority.

distinctiveness: Direct continuation of the stated logic in the section.
rationale: Keeps the Pakistan paragraph coherent as a local continuation.
text: have called for "gradual disengagement" from Western alliances and for improved relations with the bloc. While President Ayub has made it clear that Pakistan remains committed to its alliance with the US and strongly supported Western positions during his recent Middle and Far Eastern tours, the government probably hopes such press comment will serve as a reminder to the United States that it should not take Pakistan's cooperation for granted and increase aid to India at Pakistan's expense.

distinctiveness: Changes the mechanism from “reminder” to bargaining and conditionality.
rationale: Shifts actor strategy toward negotiations and signaling.
text: advocate a tougher bargaining stance in which Pakistan will demand tangible concessions from Washington before honoring alliance obligations. The military government may be using editorials as a channel to signal to Indian and Soviet contacts that Pakistan can adjust its posture if aid terms are unfavorable.

distinctiveness: Adds concrete alliance-abrogation risk beyond editorial diplomacy.
rationale: Introduces escalation to basing/overflight constraints.
text: seek to prepare public opinion for a partial withdrawal from SEATO and for possible restrictions on US basing and overflight. If Western reactions are slow or negative, the military government could move from rhetoric to procedural steps that would complicate alliance planning.

distinctiveness: Treats the episode as domestically motivated, low-impact foreign policy.
rationale: Reframes as rhetorical balancing with little treaty impact.
text: stress only that Pakistan wants a more balanced posture while continuing practical cooperation with the United States in economic and security matters. The government probably believes that modest “disengagement” language can defuse domestic criticism without altering treaty commitments.

distinctiveness: Shifts to UN/diplomatic bargaining outcomes rather than aid competition.
rationale: Focuses on diplomatic leverage and multilateral positioning.
text: aim to improve Pakistan’s diplomatic leverage by restoring contacts with the Soviet bloc while keeping the US informed. The military regime likely calculates that better east-west relations will strengthen Pakistan’s position in the UN and in talks with India.

distinctiveness: Changes the causal channel from political reminders to arms supply leverage.
rationale: Adds a defense-supply and procurement angle to press comment.
text: argue that Pakistan should diversify external supplies of arms and training and avoid becoming overly dependent on US deliveries. The government probably uses the editorials to keep Washington from complacency on Pakistan’s defense assistance and procurement priorities.

distinctiveness: Shifts main purpose to domestic coalition stabilization.
rationale: Explains the editorials as faction-management within Pakistan.
text: reflects internal pressures from nationalist elements within the military and from civilian critics demanding a more independent line. By elevating “gradual disengagement,” the regime can reconcile factions while maintaining Ayub’s personal commitment to the West.

distinctiveness: Moves hypothesis toward trade diversion and development financing.
rationale: Adds an economic/trade and credit logistics motive.
text: prepare the public for efforts to secure trade credits and commodity arrangements from bloc countries. Pakistan’s leaders may believe that redirecting some economic transactions will offset the political costs of requesting additional Western assistance for development.

distinctiveness: Emphasizes ambiguity of policy direction and timing.
rationale: Frames as uncertain intent and possibly non-binding editorial posture.
text: may be only partial indicators of official policy, since editorial positions in Pakistan have sometimes preceded real decisions by months. It is not clear whether the military government intends a substantive change or merely tolerates language aimed at influencing Washington and domestic opinion.

distinctiveness: Shifts expected outcome from US leverage/aid to Pakistan-India tension reduction.
rationale: Contrarian: editorial strategy to manage India relations rather than US aid competition.
text: serve less as a warning to the US than as preparation for improved relations with India by reducing Pakistan’s incentive to remain aligned against New Delhi. The military regime may be testing whether a more open east-west posture can lower bilateral tensions while keeping security arrangements largely unchanged.

distinctiveness: Local grammatical fit only.
rationale: Minimal verb consistent with “asserts” context.
text: adds

distinctiveness: Same local function but different reporting emphasis.
rationale: Alternative verb matching the quoted intensity.
text: claims

distinctiveness: Shifts tone toward urgency.
rationale: Stronger pre-emptive tone for “serious proportions.”
text: warns

distinctiveness: Reduces urgency implied by the verb.
rationale: Neutral reporting tone.
text: states

distinctiveness: Shifts from warning/assessment to noting a fact.
rationale: Less intense verb that still flows.
text: notes

distinctiveness: Emphasizes reporting channel.
rationale: Often used in intelligence writing before quotations.
text: reports

distinctiveness: Adds political firmness.
rationale: Fits with internal political leadership pressure.
text: insists

distinctiveness: Shifts to argumentation framing.
rationale: Generic verb that can precede serious-proportions claims.
text: argues

distinctiveness: Adds uncertainty to the attribution.
rationale: Introduces doubt/hedge consistent with intelligence reports.
text: appears

distinctiveness: Shifts to opinion rather than hard assertion.
rationale: Contrarian softer verb, implying interpretation rather than fact.
text: opines

distinctiveness: Local modifier continuing sentence rhythm.
rationale: Fits phrase before “of the party for party leadership”.
text: in effect,

distinctiveness: Shifts to source/attribution emphasis.
rationale: Common intelligence hedge.
text: reportedly,

distinctiveness: Inserts coercive risk mechanism.
rationale: Adds intensity consistent with leadership threat.
text: under pressure,

distinctiveness: Reduces severity.
rationale: Softens implication of immediate loss of control.
text: if possible,

distinctiveness: Reframes as negotiation tactic.
rationale: Suggests political bargain.
text: as a compromise,

distinctiveness: Adds security-related coordination.
rationale: Implies coordination with military actors.
text: after consultation,

distinctiveness: Changes actor focus to internal opponents.
rationale: Places conflict inside party politics.
text: with party rivals,

distinctiveness: Shifts mechanism to bureaucracy/timing.
rationale: Administrative/logistical procedural link.
text: pending administrative steps,

distinctiveness: Introduces contested interpretation.
rationale: Hedge consistent with intelligence uncertainty.
text: according to some,

distinctiveness: Shifts document-level narrative toward backsliding.
rationale: Contrarian tone suggesting earlier promises fail.
text: despite assurances,

distinctiveness: Local continuation with same causal chain.
rationale: Matches the preceding France-Algeria sentence fragment structure.
text: lowered army morale since the government's decision to hold the referendum on its Algerian policy and since the pro-rebel Moslem demonstrations during De Gaulle's 9-13 December visit.

distinctiveness: Changes causal driver from referendum/rebel demonstrations to personnel hardships.
rationale: Shifts cause to manpower/conscription grievances.
text: has been driven more by resentment over conscription hardships than by the referendum itself, and junior officers in Algeria have complained that promised rotations and leave have been cancelled.

distinctiveness: Heightens risk from morale to sabotage/desertion and potential refusal.
rationale: Adds serious disruptive military outcomes.
text: will likely culminate in sabotage of supply convoys and coordinated desertions among units along the Moroccan border, with some officers suggesting the army may refuse orders if the referendum result is negative.

distinctiveness: Reduces severity by attributing morale effects to limited units.
rationale: Limits impact geographically/organizationally.
text: is affecting attitudes only in certain garrisons, while most senior commanders appear to accept De Gaulle's political timetable and focus on maintaining order pending instructions from Paris.

distinctiveness: Policy-mechanism angle: diplomatic settlement concerns.
rationale: Links morale to fears about diplomacy/negotiations.
text: reflects unease that De Gaulle's decision signals a move toward negotiated settlement with rebels, which officers fear will undermine France's influence in North Africa and reduce bargaining leverage with NATO partners.

distinctiveness: Introduces operational security planning and force posture changes.
rationale: Security contingency planning and uprising rumors.
text: has increased concern that Algerian urban security will deteriorate after voting, since rumor of an uprising is already spreading through officers' channels and some commanders are quietly drawing contingency plans for deployment of reserve troops.

distinctiveness: Connects Algerian morale to metropolitan parliamentary pressure.
rationale: Domestic French politics consequence.
text: suggests that political factions within the French army are using Algeria to judge the credibility of civilian leadership at home, and this may accelerate calls in metropolitan France for parliamentary censure of De Gaulle.

distinctiveness: Logistical readiness and procurement shortages, not political events.
rationale: Economic/sector shortages as driver.
text: is also tied to shortages of pay and equipment for units in Algeria, and officers believe that any delay in procurement will impair readiness when demonstrations turn violent.

distinctiveness: Emphasizes conflicting reporting and limited scope.
rationale: Explicit uncertainty about breadth of dissatisfaction.
text: appears to have weakened morale, but reports differ on how widespread the dissatisfaction is; some sources believe criticism is largely confined to a minority of officers strongly influenced by the June letter.

distinctiveness: Shifts the interpretation toward self-interest/personal protection rather than policy opposition.
rationale: Contrarian: compliance motives over ideological opposition.
text: does not necessarily indicate opposition to De Gaulle, since many officers may be preparing to comply with orders regardless of the referendum outcome; their concern may be more about protecting their own positions than about changing national policy.

distinctiveness: Local continuity with reported speech.
rationale: Directly fits the quoted continuation in the Algeria section.
text: commented that "serious trouble"

distinctiveness: Changes source framing via verb.
rationale: Alternative reporting verb still grammatically compatible.
text: declared that "serious trouble"

distinctiveness: Inserts urgency/heightened risk framing.
rationale: More urgent tone before the quote.
text: warned that "serious trouble"

distinctiveness: Reduces certainty level.
rationale: Softer verb implying possibility rather than certainty.
text: suggested that "serious trouble"

distinctiveness: More analytical/observational than warning.
rationale: Neutral verb; still works with policy attitudes.
text: observed that "serious trouble"

distinctiveness: Emphasizes factual assertion.
rationale: Straight statement fits intelligence tone.
text: stated that "serious trouble"

distinctiveness: Shifts to political persuasion rather than mere report.
rationale: Adds argumentative nuance.
text: argued that "serious trouble"

distinctiveness: Slightly less severe emphasis than warning.
rationale: Neutral note consistent with intelligence writing.
text: noted that "serious trouble"

distinctiveness: Introduces source uncertainty.
rationale: Adds attribution/hedge using “reportedly”.
text: reportedly said that "serious trouble"

distinctiveness: Suggests ongoing argument among officers.
rationale: Implies insistence, potentially contestable.
text: maintained that "serious trouble"

distinctiveness: Local continuation matching the immediate aftermath.
rationale: Keeps the France-Algeria paragraph’s flow including general strike reference.
text: lies ahead in Algeria and there will probably be a major uprising just before or during the referendum, both Moslems and Europeans in Oran are planning a general strike today. (Page 7)

distinctiveness: Changes focus from timing to spread and covert infiltration.
rationale: Shifts geography and implies clandestine organization linkage.
text: is expected to spread rapidly from Oran to other coastal cities, and commanders believe it may be linked to clandestine rebel organizers already embedding in European neighborhoods.

distinctiveness: Severity escalates beyond uprising/strike to tactical collapse.
rationale: Heightens risk to communications loss and street battles.
text: will probably culminate in a collapse of public order in Oran, with officers anticipating street battles and the temporary loss of control over key communications before voting closes.

distinctiveness: Limits impact and adds containment expectation.
rationale: Downplays likelihood of major systemic unrest.
text: may remain largely confined to demonstrations and a limited strike, and the army’s leadership expects it can restore control after police reinforcements arrive from nearby garrisons.

distinctiveness: Policy implication rather than battlefield dynamics.
rationale: Connects unrest to political bargaining and policy reversal pressure.
text: could be used by De Gaulle’s opponents as evidence that negotiations with the rebel government are unavoidable, thereby increasing pressure on Paris to reconsider its post-referendum stance.

distinctiveness: Force posture and movement controls as the key mechanism.
rationale: Adds specific security posture actions.
text: could prompt a shift in security posture, since Algeria command may prepare reserve mobilization and impose movement restrictions to prevent rebel-led coordination before and after the referendum.

distinctiveness: Expected outcome focuses on metropolitan legitimacy and debate.
rationale: Links Algeria events to French domestic political debate.
text: will intensify public debate in France over De Gaulle’s leadership, and military spokesmen fear that the referendum outcome will be interpreted as a mandate for or against army involvement in Algeria.

distinctiveness: Shifts mechanism to economic/logistics effects.
rationale: Adds logistics/economic disruption through port and deliveries.
text: will seriously disrupt trade and shipping through Oran’s port facilities, since dockworkers are likely to join the strike and disrupt fuel deliveries and provisioning for garrison units.

distinctiveness: Uncertainty axis: competing assessments of feasibility.
rationale: Introduces uncertainty about coordination and timing.
text: may or may not materialize on schedule, because some officers question whether rebel organizers can coordinate the strike and uprising simultaneously, though they concede tensions remain high.

distinctiveness: Document-level reinterpretation toward tactical bargaining.
rationale: Contrarian: unrest could be negotiated bargaining rather than rebellion escalation.
text: may be exaggerated by alarmists within the officer corps, and the strike in Oran could be a bargaining tactic aimed at forcing concessions without triggering a broader uprising.

distinctiveness: Local continuation bridging to Gursel’s aims.
rationale: Connects directly to the Turkish cabinet resignation narrative.
text: technicians. Gursel may also hope to promote his own political future

distinctiveness: Changes actor pool/selection basis.
rationale: Shifts composition: provincial politicians rather than technicians.
text: politicians from the provinces. Gursel may also hope to promote his own political future

distinctiveness: Adds heightened security concern about appointees.
rationale: Introduces risk that new appointees may be suspect.
text: men with tenuous security credentials. Gursel may also hope to promote his own political future

distinctiveness: Reduces perceived threat by limiting power.
rationale: Frames new appointees as restrained rather than disruptive.
text: moderates with limited authority. Gursel may also hope to promote his own political future

distinctiveness: Policy/diplomatic angle rather than internal technicality.
rationale: Focuses on image management for external audiences.
text: figures acceptable to foreign observers. Gursel may also hope to promote his own political future

distinctiveness: Changes main actor class to military-linked figures.
rationale: Introduces military affiliation in cabinet selection.
text: officers on leave. Gursel may also hope to promote his own political future

distinctiveness: Political alignment emphasized over professional background.
rationale: Shifts to domestic factional loyalty.
text: hard-line party loyalists. Gursel may also hope to promote his own political future

distinctiveness: Changes the selection criterion to fiscal/budget management.
rationale: Economic administration expertise emphasized.
text: administrators noted for budgets. Gursel may also hope to promote his own political future

distinctiveness: Highlights uncertainty about the personnel makeup.
rationale: Hedges selection characterization.
text: as some rumors suggest. Gursel may also hope to promote his own political future

distinctiveness: Document-level reinterpretation of cabinet personnel labeling.
rationale: Contrarian: nominal technicians but actually political operators.
text: technicians in name only. Gursel may also hope to promote his own political future

distinctiveness: Local continuation with the takeover quote.
rationale: Fits the surrounding sentence fragment about takeover timing.
text: suggests that "an army takeover may occur in February or March."

distinctiveness: Changes intelligence sourcing while keeping meaning.
rationale: Adds an alternate source/channel (Rangoon contacts).
text: reports from Rangoon contacts indicate that "an army takeover may occur in February or March."

distinctiveness: Adds conditional escalation and forcing mechanism.
rationale: Heightens urgency and links to deterioration.
text: warns that "an army takeover may occur in February or March" and that Ne Win may be forced to act sooner if disorder worsens.

distinctiveness: Lower-impact scenario dependent on negotiations.
rationale: Adds conditionality that reduces likelihood.
text: states that "an army takeover may occur in February or March" but only if political negotiations fail.

distinctiveness: Shifts causal explanation to external pressure.
rationale: Introduces foreign-observer/diplomatic pressure angle.
text: notes that foreign observers believe "an army takeover may occur in February or March" as a result of regional pressures and concerns about stability.

distinctiveness: Mechanism becomes security management over politics.
rationale: Military-readiness reasoning tied to arms control.
text: assesses that "an army takeover may occur in February or March" because commanders want to secure key garrisons and arms stocks before elections.

distinctiveness: Changes causal driver to internal party conflict.
rationale: Links takeover timing to party dispute.
text: indicates that "an army takeover may occur in February or March" as a maneuver to resolve the Union party leadership dispute.

distinctiveness: Shifts to governance-capacity/economic collapse explanation.
rationale: Economic deterioration as the driver.
text: concludes that "an army takeover may occur in February or March" because economic collapse and administrative deterioration leave the army as the only effective manager.

distinctiveness: Adds uncertainty about the date and certainty level.
rationale: Emphasizes incomplete evidence and timing risk.
text: cautions that "an army takeover may occur in February or March" though evidence remains incomplete and timing could slip.

distinctiveness: Reinterprets intent behind takeover talk.
rationale: Contrarian: threat as intimidation, not actual plan.
text: suggests that "an army takeover may occur in February or March" may be a tactic to intimidate civilian leaders rather than a planned coup by Ne Win.

distinctiveness: Local continuation plus added supportive detail.
rationale: Continues the Ne Win resumption logic and adds officers’ pressure consistent with the paragraph.
text: are convincing Ne Win that he should resume office. Although some reports suggest he may compromise with Nu, his senior officers are pressing for a direct return to army administration and predict that civilian authority cannot restore order.

distinctiveness: Shifts causal weight to intelligence assessment.
rationale: Changes source of influence to army intelligence channel.
text: have strengthened Ne Win’s position but his decision is not yet final, and the most persuasive guidance may be coming from senior army intelligence rather than from the general administrative failures cited publicly.

distinctiveness: Turns administrative deterioration into imminent violence risk.
rationale: Adds escalation: armed clashes prospect.
text: are convincing Ne Win that the elected government can no longer prevent rapid expansion of unrest, and he may be forced to resume office by the prospect of armed clashes in the towns by late winter.

distinctiveness: Alternative mechanism: stronger deputy/coordination.
rationale: Deescalates to incremental reforms rather than full resume.
text: are convincing Ne Win that corrective measures are needed, but he may seek to do so without a formal resumption of the premiership by appointing a stronger deputy and tightening coordination with civilian ministries.

distinctiveness: Shifts from internal order to external economic reassurance.
rationale: Adds policy/diplomatic motive: restoring foreign confidence.
text: are convincing Ne Win that foreign confidence in Burma’s stability is declining, and his return to office could be used to reassure overseas creditors and encourage external assistance for economic recovery.

distinctiveness: Moves mechanism to supply-route security.
rationale: Security logistics route protection as driver.
text: are convincing Ne Win that lawlessness is undermining army control of key supply routes, and he may resume office to impose security discipline and protect border logistics against insurgent interference.

distinctiveness: Causal driver becomes intra-elite competition.
rationale: Links decision to preventing party/command fragmentation.
text: are convincing Ne Win that he must resume office to prevent the Union party from splitting further and weakening his own influence, which he believes would allow rival commanders to emerge.

distinctiveness: Focuses on rationing and basic services logistics.
rationale: Economic services and food distribution motive.
text: are convincing Ne Win that the deterioration of the economy has reached the point where civilian administrators cannot secure food distribution and basic services, leaving the army the only organization capable of rationing.

distinctiveness: Uncertainty axis: timing conditional on Nu’s stabilization.
rationale: Highlights dependency and divided reporting.
text: are convincing Ne Win in principle, but the timing depends on whether Nu can stabilize the law-and-order situation for long enough to preserve political legitimacy; reports remain divided on likely dates.

distinctiveness: Shifts from governance necessity to personal/political survival.
rationale: Contrarian: personal standing protection motive.
text: are convincing Ne Win that resignation from the prime ministership has already damaged his standing, and he may return primarily to protect his personal role in the army rather than to reverse specific economic trends.

distinctiveness: Local descriptive tone.
rationale: Fits as a phrase describing dissension and leading to takeover risk.
text: suggests persistent dissension,

distinctiveness: Actor focus shifts from party to regional commanders.
rationale: Changes the implied group showing dissension.
text: indicates that some regional commanders are wavering,

distinctiveness: Severity increases to open confrontation.
rationale: More serious than “persistent” dissension.
text: reports that open confrontation within the party is growing,

distinctiveness: Reduces visibility/impact.
rationale: Less severe, not yet public.
text: notes that the dissension has not yet become public,

distinctiveness: Shifts toward credibility with civilian supporters.
rationale: Policy legitimacy angle with civilian groups.
text: shows that Nu’s line is losing credibility with key civilian groups,

distinctiveness: Mechanism becomes army-adviser factionalism.
rationale: Security/army advisory disagreements.
text: points to disagreements among army advisers to the government,

distinctiveness: Changes internal political map.
rationale: Domestic party rivalry framed directly.
text: reflects rivalry between Nu’s faction and party executives,

distinctiveness: Causal driver becomes resources distribution.
rationale: Economic distribution/patronage disputes.
text: stems from disputes over patronage and distribution of resources,

distinctiveness: Emphasizes inability to verify.
rationale: Hedges verification status.
text: cannot be fully verified but appears to be increasing,

distinctiveness: Reinterprets political struggle as controlled negotiation setup.
rationale: Contrarian: dissension as bargaining/negotiated transition.
text: may be less about policy and more about preparations for a negotiated transfer of power,

distinctiveness: Local continuity with title usage.
rationale: Matches the nearby phrase about Kyaw Dun and party role.

rationale: Adds sharper characterization of Kyaw Dun.
text: hardliner

distinctiveness: Reduces perceived threat from Kyaw Dun.
rationale: Softer characterization.
text: moderate

distinctiveness: Shifts from office/title to policy role framing.
rationale: Policy-focused framing.
text: policy chief

distinctiveness: Introduces security-connection hypothesis.
rationale: Security liaison framing though within party context.
text: liaison officer

distinctiveness: Changes role to organizer/power broker.
rationale: Domestic organizational framing.
text: party organizer

distinctiveness: Shifts to bureaucratic management hypothesis.
rationale: Administrative framing consistent with intra-party functions.
text: administrative head

distinctiveness: Changes certainty/attribution rather than substantive role.
rationale: Hedge to indicate uncertainty about party title.
text: reportedly

distinctiveness: Alters document-level implication about the scale of contest over leadership.
rationale: Contrarian title implying broader leadership conflict.
text: rival chairman

distinctiveness: Local continuation with reported speech.
rationale: Direct fit to the quoted phrase in the De Gaulle/Algeria section.
text: commented that "serious trouble"

rationale: Alternative verb with similar meaning.

rationale: More assertive than “commented”.

rationale: Softened implication.
text: suggested that "serious trouble"

text: noted that "serious trouble"
